sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Jerry, a 45-year-old male, presents with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son.
He reports landing awkwardly after being fouled and twisting his ankle, resulting in pain and swelling on the outside, which is bruised and swollen.
He has been having trouble walking and feels the ankle is not stable.
He recently started working out and is concerned about disrupting his routine.
His son is doing well and is enthusiastic about basketball.
Jerry is a coach and will start coaching in April.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: Examination of the ankle reveals exquisite tenderness to palpation laterally and a little bit medially, swelling on the lateral side, no epidermolysis, intact skin, brisk capillary refill, and no malalignment.
The patient has difficulty walking on the ankle due to instability.
Test Results: An x-ray of the right ankle shows a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Jerry presents with a displaced lateral malleolar fracture, which is unstable and requires surgical intervention.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; Suspected instability of the ankle.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain management, and I advised Jerry to ice the ankle and keep it elevated.
I also provided him with crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle.
Jerry will continue coaching in April and will need to take a three-month leave from exercise to allow the ankle to heal.
Follow-up: Jerry will be seen again in three months to assess the healing of the ankle fracture.
